why.plain_0: I'm going to tell you the worst due-diligence story I know. It's mine.
why.plain_1: If you're about to buy a SaaS business, read this before you wire a single dollar.
why.plain_2: Two years ago, I found a SaaS business listed at $1.1M. $42K MRR. Three years of growth.
why.plain_3: The CIM showed a clean 2.3% monthly churn rate. The seller was transparent — or so I thought.
why.plain_4: He handed over a ProfitBird summary, cohort charts, and a churn dashboard screenshot. Everything
why.plain_5: looked healthy. My due diligence checklist had 40 boxes. I checked all 40. The deal closed.
why.plain_6: I was a careful buyer. I'd read every SaaS acquisition guide. I knew the right questions to ask.
why.plain_7: I asked them. I got good answers. I trusted the numbers because the numbers were presented by
why.plain_8: someone who understood how to present numbers.
why.plain_9: Four months post-close, MRR started declining. Not crashing — bleeding. Slow, steady,
why.plain_10: the kind of decline you don't notice until it's too late. I pulled the subscription data and
why.plain_11: ran the churn calculation myself for the first time.
why.plain_12: The real monthly churn wasn't 2.3%. It was 9.4%. Over four times what the CIM reported. That gap — the difference between the number I trusted and the number that was real — was worth $340,000.
why.plain_13: How did the seller get 2.3% when the real number was 9.4%? He hadn't lied. He'd used every
why.plain_14: legitimate-looking methodology trick available: he excluded downgrades from churn (revenue
why.plain_15: disappeared but the logo stayed). He reported the best cohort month as "representative." He
why.plain_16: excluded involuntary cancellations (failed payments). He counted reactivations as continuous
why.plain_17: subscriptions. Every single one of these choices is defensible in isolation. Combined, they
why.text_19: I hadn't asked for the raw CSV. I'd asked for a churn number. I got the number the
why.plain_20: seller wanted me to have. And I paid for it.
why.plain_21: After that deal, I did something obsessive. I pulled the raw subscription CSV from every SaaS
why.plain_22: acquisition opportunity I evaluated — not the summary, not the dashboard, the raw export
why.plain_23: with customer ID, MRR, plan, status, dates. I built a spreadsheet to compute churn five different
why.plain_24: ways: logo churn, revenue churn, net churn, gross churn, and cohort-adjusted churn. I cross-referenced
why.plain_25: concentration risk. I flagged inactive paying accounts.
why.plain_26: The only number that matters is the one you compute yourself from the raw data.
why.plain_27: If you're trusting a summary number someone else computed, you're not doing due diligence —
why.plain_28: I ran this analysis on 14 more deals over the next year. In 11 of them, real churn was meaningfully
why.plain_29: higher than reported. In 3, it was the same. Zero times was real churn lower. The gap averaged 4.2x.
why.plain_30: Every deal where I caught the gap, I either renegotiated the price or walked. Every deal saved me
why.plain_31: more than the cost of the analysis by a factor of 100.
why.plain_32: The spreadsheet worked, but it took me 4 hours per deal. And I kept making the same analysis
why.plain_33: manual mistakes. So I automated it. Then I showed it to two other acquirers I knew. They asked
why.plain_34: to use it. Then their friends asked. That's when I realized this wasn't a spreadsheet —
why.plain_35: it was the thing I wished I'd had before I wired $1.1M for a business worth $760K.
why.plain_36: Churn Lens is that spreadsheet, systematized. Upload the seller's raw subscription CSV. Get
why.plain_37: every churn metric computed five ways. See concentration risk, annual-plan churn exposure,
why.plain_38: inactive accounts, and a composite Revenue Quality Score — in minutes, not hours.
why.plain_39: Right now, 80% of SaaS acquisitions overpay because buyers trust seller-computed churn.
why.plain_40: The information asymmetry in SaaS M&A favors the seller at every step — they know
why.plain_41: the data, they compute the metrics, they present the summary. Churn Lens exists to flip that
why.plain_42: asymmetry. When every buyer demands the raw CSV and runs their own analysis, the era of
why.plain_43: hidden churn ends. That's the world we're building toward.
why.plain_44: If you're evaluating a SaaS acquisition right now, you probably hold at least one of these beliefs.
why.plain_45: I held all three. They cost me $340K.
why.text_46: FALSE BELIEF #1 (Vehicle): "The seller's churn methodology is industry-standard, so the number is reliable."Reality: There is no standard. Every seller makes methodology choices that flatter their number. The only reliable number is the one you compute from raw data.
why.text_47: FALSE BELIEF #2 (Internal): "I'm not technical enough to analyze raw subscription data myself."Reality: You don't need to be. You need the CSV and a tool that computes churn five ways. The analysis takes minutes, not a finance degree.
why.text_48: FALSE BELIEF #3 (External): "The seller wouldn't hide churn from me — they'd get caught in the earnout."Reality: Most churn methodology choices are legal, defensible, and invisible. Earnouts don't protect you from legitimate-looking accounting that understates real churn by 4x.
why.plain_49: Already have a target in due diligence?

who.text_0: Alex has bought two SaaS businesses before. The first one worked out. The second one had "2.3% reported churn" that turned out to be 9.4% — a $340K mistake they caught too late. Now they know: the seller's churn number isn't the truth, it's a negotiation starting point.

manifesto.plain_0: SaaS M&A has an information asymmetry problem. The seller knows the data, computes the metrics,
manifesto.plain_1: and presents the summary. The buyer trusts. This manifesto is the line in the sand.
manifesto.plain_2: When you buy a SaaS business, the seller hands you a CIM with a churn number. That number
manifesto.plain_3: is computed using methodology choices the seller made. Every choice — whether to include
manifesto.plain_4: downgrades, how to handle involuntary cancellations, which cohort to report — is legally
manifesto.plain_5: defensible. Every choice happens to make the number smaller.
manifesto.text_6: 80% of SaaS acquisitions overpay because of this gap. The average real churn
manifesto.plain_7: is 4.2x the reported number. On a $1M deal, that's $340K of evaporated value. The buyer never
manifesto.plain_8: sees it coming because the methodology choices are invisible in a summary metric.
manifesto.text_9: This isn't an improvement on the old way. It's a completely different approach.
manifesto.plain_10: The old way asks "what does the seller report?" The new way asks "what does the raw data actually
manifesto.plain_11: show?" Those are fundamentally different questions with fundamentally different answers.
manifesto.plain_12: The long-term vision: a SaaS acquisition market where buyer-side churn analysis is the default,
manifesto.plain_13: not the exception. Where no buyer trusts a summary churn number. Where sellers know that any
manifesto.plain_14: methodology tricks will be caught in minutes by automated analysis. When that happens, the
manifesto.plain_15: information asymmetry closes. Deals get priced honestly. Buyers stop overpaying.
manifesto.plain_16: Churn Lens is the tool. The checklist is the entry point. The raw-data methodology is the principle.
manifesto.plain_17: And the movement is every acquirer who reads this and demands the CSV on their next deal.

partners.plain_8: Most SaaS acquisition due diligence is theater. You trust a summary number the seller computed using their own methodology.
partners.text_10: 80% of SaaS acquirers overpay because they trust the seller's churn number.
partners.plain_11: I was one of them. The deal looked perfect on paper — 2.3% reported churn, growing MRR, sticky customer base.
partners.plain_12: The real churn was 9.4%. The gap cost me $340K.
partners.plain_13: Here's what I learned: there's no "industry standard" for churn calculation. Every seller chooses a methodology that flatters their number. The only fix is to compute it yourself from raw data.
partners.plain_14: That's why I built Churn Lens — a buyer-side tool that catches hidden churn, concentration risk, and revenue decay sellers don't disclose.
partners.text_16: No finance degree required. Just the CSV and 10 minutes.
partners.text_17: Thinking of buying a SaaS business? Don't trust the churn number in the CIM.

partners.plain_21: No minimums. No lock-in. Just great commissions and a tool your audience actually needs.

customer-concentration-risk.text_0: Real-world example: A Churn Lens user was evaluating a $2.4M ARR SaaS target that "sounded diversified" — ~300 customers. The CSV showed Customer A at $58K MRR (29% of total), Customer B at $41K MRR (20.5%), and Customer C at $27K MRR (13.5%). Three customers = 63% of revenue. The deal fell apart during diligence when Customer A confirmed they were planning to migrate off the platform.

saas-mrr-decline-analysis.text_0: Real case from Churn Lens: A $2.1M ARR target showed 3% month-over-month MRR growth. Churn Lens computed organic MRR: the company was losing 4.2% of revenue every month to churn and contraction. Without $73K/month in new sales, the business would lose half its revenue in 14 months. The buyer re-priced the deal with a 2.1× multiple instead of 4.5×.

saas-revenue-churn-calculator.text_0: Key insight: The seller would probably quote "4% monthly churn" and "95% NRR." Both are true. But the revenue churn is 8% — double the logo churn — because the customers who left were higher-value than average. This is a concentration-quality problem that Churn Lens flags automatically.
